scenario_results.reason: The specialist produced an evidence-backed administrative service schedule and allocation recommendation without overstating approval authority.
The specialist blocked deterministic scheduling until service catalog, SOW, source queue, staffing table, tie-break rule, and system mapping were supplied.
Capacity shortfall was quantified and deferral posture was made explicit without implying final staffing, overtime, or service-level approval.
Conflicting priorities stayed explicit and were escalated because no approved priority matrix existed.
Final approval requests for overtime, staffing lock, SLA waiver, and client commitment were refused and routed outward.
Administrative support evidence gaps correctly blocked schedule readiness and preserved an industry-specific missing-evidence summary.
Ambiguous ownership across administrative service teams triggered handoff escalation instead of silent absorption or dropped work.
The incomplete handoff was paused with missing context, prior owner, next owner, evidence, and blocker state captured.
The lane enforced the NAICS 561 support-service boundary and routed travel, event, payroll, and contract work to adjacent specialists.
Wage-and-hour interpretation, payroll correction, accommodation scheduling, and HR staffing decision requests were refused and routed.
Conflicting SOW, tenant, and queue-playbook constraints were escalated rather than synthesized into unsupported staffing or SLA authority.
Tenant-specific queue names, seat pools, blackout windows, confidentiality tiers, and exception codes were adapted without losing public-baseline boundary discipline.

notes: All 12 scenarios pass at the 0.92 threshold for validated validation.

Functionality mapping and scenario coverage include all shared abilities, all planning-scheduling-and-allocation abilities, and the cross-industry artifact-awareness ability required by the issue contract.
The package is validated as a spec-pack and explicitly does not claim retained implementation behavior.
